Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty hands-on and scattered. I'd spend a lot of hours studying each week, maybe around 33 hours, but it wasn't always at a desk with the LMS open. I learn best by doing things physically—writing notes by hand, walking around while reviewing concepts, or practicing problems on paper. With limited resources at home, I had to be creative about how I studied, often downloading what I could and working offline.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot. Some weeks I'd be grinding hard because I felt motivated or a deadline was coming, and other weeks life just got in the way. Stress was a constant thing for me, and when it peaked, I'd have to step back and do some mindfulness exercises just to get through. So no, there wasn't really a fixed schedule—it was more about surviving each week as it came.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I absolutely needed something—like checking an assignment deadline or downloading materials I couldn't access any other way. I didn't browse around the LMS for fun or anything like that. With my internet situation not always being reliable and resources being tight, I tried to get in, grab what I needed, and get out quickly.
Q4: I wouldn't say the LMS ever became a natural part of my routine, to be honest. It felt more like a tool I used when necessary rather than something I engaged with daily. The few times it felt routine were during the first weeks of the course when everything was new, but that faded as I figured out I could study more effectively offline with downloaded materials.
Q5: The video lectures were actually really useful—I watched a lot of them, probably close to five hours worth. Being a kinesthetic learner, I'd watch them while taking handwritten notes or even acting out processes to understand them better. The discussion boards were okay too, but I only posted a couple times because I preferred talking to peers directly when I could.
Q6: I mostly ignored the optional readings and some of the supplementary materials. It wasn't that they weren't valuable, but with limited time and energy, I had to prioritize what would directly help me pass. Some resources also required constant internet access, which was a barrier for me. I focused on what I could download and use repeatedly offline.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see how it directly connected to assignments or exams. If I could physically engage with it somehow—like practice problems I could work through by hand—that made a huge difference. Passive reading or watching without a clear purpose felt like a waste when I was already stretched thin with stress and limited resources.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't always afford. Required ones had clear consequences if I skipped them, so those got priority. With optional stuff, I'd think 'maybe later' and then later never came because something more urgent always popped up. It's not that I didn't value them, but survival mode doesn't leave much room for extras.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my studying happened outside the LMS. I'd download PDFs, lecture slides, and videos when I had good internet, then study them offline for hours. I also used my own handwritten notes extensively and would practice problems on paper. My actual learning time was way more than what any system could track because it happened with physical materials.
Q10: There were many times I'd be deep into studying—rewriting notes, doing practice problems, even teaching concepts to myself out loud—and none of that showed up anywhere online. I might spend four hours working through downloaded materials without touching the LMS once. My engagement was real, just not digital.
Q11: Yeah, this was basically my standard approach. I'd log in once, download everything available for a module, and then not come back until I needed something new. It saved data and time, plus I could study whenever without worrying about connectivity. The LMS was like a library I'd visit to check out books, not a place I'd sit and read.
Q12: There were definitely harder periods, especially when stress got really high. Sometimes I'd feel this overwhelming sadness that made it hard to focus on anything academic. During those times, the mindfulness practices I learned really helped me push through. Financial pressures and not having reliable resources also made some weeks feel impossible.
Q13: Fatigue and stress were constant companions, honestly. When I was exhausted, I'd skip logging into the LMS entirely and just work with whatever materials I already had downloaded. Competing responsibilities—like helping out at home since resources are tight—definitely cut into my online engagement time. The LMS activity doesn't show how much I was juggling.
Q14: Sometimes I'd open a video or reading and my mind would just be elsewhere, dealing with stress or sadness. I'd technically be 'accessing' the material but not absorbing anything. Those moments were frustrating because I knew I was wasting precious time, but my brain just wouldn't cooperate. Mindfulness helped me recognize when this was happening.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts a lot because they felt less urgent than assignments with hard deadlines. I also avoided some activities when I was in a low mood—even knowing they were important, I just couldn't bring myself to engage. It wasn't laziness; it was more about emotional and mental capacity being depleted.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my main driver for LMS activity. I'd log in more frequently as due dates approached, sometimes cramming to submit things. Without a deadline, I probably wouldn't log in at all for days. It's not ideal, but when you're managing high stress and limited resources, you triage constantly.
Q17: Feedback did motivate me when I received it, but honestly, I didn't always check it promptly. When I saw that my work was decent, it gave me a boost to keep going. Negative feedback was harder—it sometimes added to my stress. But overall, knowing someone was looking at my work made the effort feel more worthwhile.
Q18: The course structure was okay, but it assumed everyone had consistent access and energy, which wasn't my reality. Weekly modules helped me know what to prioritize, but the pacing sometimes felt relentless when life got complicated. I appreciated having materials available for download—that flexibility was crucial for someone in my situation.
Q19: Having downloadable content made engagement much easier for me. What made it harder was when activities required real-time participation or constant connectivity. Also, the sheer volume of materials sometimes felt overwhelming when I was already stressed. Simpler, more focused content would have helped me engage more consistently.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for things, trying to understand what's expected, or just feeling lost. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity—sometimes you're clicking around because you're confused or anxious. Others might be dealing with personal stuff that prevents them from actually completing work even though they're trying.
Q21: That actually sounds a lot like me. My LMS activity was pretty low, but I still passed because I was putting in serious work offline. I studied a ton with downloaded materials and handwritten notes. The system couldn't see my 33 hours of weekly studying because most of it happened away from the screen.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they miss the depth of actual learning. They can't measure understanding, effort, or engagement with offline materials. For someone like me who learns kinesthetically and studies mostly offline, the logs probably make me look way less engaged than I actually was.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions. It's about wrestling with concepts until they make sense, asking questions even if just to yourself, and connecting what you learn to something real. For me, engagement happens in my head and hands, not necessarily on a screen.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my resilience—how I pushed through high stress and sadness to still pass this course. It wouldn't show the mindfulness sessions that kept me going, or the hours I spent with physical notes and downloaded materials. It definitely wouldn't capture how much effort it took to stay engaged with limited resources and a lot of life pressure.
